its equivalent/substitute
Janani-Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK)
JSSK provides completely free and cashless services to pregnant women including normal 
deliveries and caesarean operations and free drugs, diagnostic, treatment and transport facility 
upto one year after birth in government health institutions in both rural and urban areas.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
health institutions in both rural and urban areas. Beneficiaries: Pregnant women who access government health facilities for their delivery
Benefits 
• Entitlements for pregnant women: 
• Free and cashless delivery; free C-Section and provision of blood
• Free – drugs, diagnostics and diet during stay in the health institutions, 
• Exemption from user charges
• Free transport from home to health institutions, between facilities in case of referral and 
drop back from Institutions to home after 48 hrs stay
• Entitlements for sick newborn and infants till one year after birth: 
• Free – drugs, diagnostics and treatment
• Free transport from home to health institutions, between facilities in case of referral and 
a free drop Back from Institutions to home
Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyaan
It is a fixed day strategy, every month across the country.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
fixed day strategy, every month across the country. This scheme provides a minimum 
package of ante-natal care services to the beneficiaries on the 9th day of every month at

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
48
the Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Clinics. If the 9th day of the month is a Sunday/
a holiday, then the Clinic should be organized on the next working day.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
should be organized on the next working day. Beneficiaries: All pregnant women in second and third trimesters of pregnancy
Benefits
This scheme provides a comprehensive 
package of ante-natal care services to the 
beneficiaries on the 9th day of every month 
at the  Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva 
Clinics to ensure that every pregnant 
woman receives at least one check-up in the 
2nd/3rd trimester of pregnancy.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
one check-up in the 2nd/3rd trimester of pregnancy. Maternity Leave
Paid maternity leave to women employees of 
26 weeks, of this a maximum of 8 weeks can 
be availed before the expected due date and 
the remaining after delivery. Women with 2 or more living children can 
avail 12 weeks of paid maternity leave. 6 
weeks before the expected due date and 6 
weeks after delivery.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
53
Up to 6 Months of Age
Do
• Breastfeed as often as the child wants, day and night, at least 8 times in 24 hours
• Do not give any other foods or fluids not even water
Remember
• Continue breastfeeding if the child is sick, breastfeed more frequently
6 up to 9 Months
Do
• Breastfeed as often as the child wants
• Initiate complementary feeding at 6 months
• Start by giving 2 to 3 tablespoons of soft semi-solid food. Gradually increase to 1/2 cups (1 cup=250ml).

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
food. Gradually increase to 1/2 cups (1 cup=250ml). • Mashed roti/rice mixed in undiluted milk OR thick dal with added ghee/oil OR khichri with added oil/ghee
• Add cooked vegetables also in the servings, OR 
• Sevian/dalia/halwa/kheer prepared in milk, OR 
• Any cereal porridge cooked in milk, OR 
• Mashed boiled potatoes
• Introduce one new food at a time such as khichri, dalia
• Increase quantity and diversity of the feeds
• Include at least 4 food groups such as: 1) cereals, 2) green vegetables and fruits, 3) Oil, ghee 4) mashed 
dal/fish/egg (only hard-boiled)
• Give 2 to 3 meals each day.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
• Give 2 to 3 meals each day. Offer 1 or 2 snacks each day between meals when the child seems hungry
Remember
• Keep the child in your lap and feed with your own hands 
• Wash your own and child’s hands with soap and water every time before and after feeding
9 up to 12 Months
Do
• Breastfeed as often as the child wants
• Give at least half cup serving* of a variety of foods at a time 
• Mashed roti/rice mixed in undiluted milk, OR
• Mashed roti/rice mixed in thick dal with added ghee/oil or khichri with added oil/ghee.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
with added ghee/oil or khichri with added oil/ghee. • Add cooked vegetables also in the servings OR
• Sevian/dalia/halwa/kheer prepared in milk, OR
• Any cereal porridge cooked in milk, OR
• Mashed boiled potatoes
• Feed at least half katori of food that requires chewing – 3 to 4 times a day 
• Give 3 to 4 meals each day
• Offer 1 or 2 snacks between meals. The child will eat if hungry.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
between meals. The child will eat if hungry. • For snacks, give small chewable items that the child can hold
• Let your child try to eat the snack but help her/him if needed
Remember
• Keep the child in your lap and feed with your own hands
• Wash your own and child’s hands with soap and water every time before and after feeding
Annexure 8 
Feeding Recommendations

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
54
12 Months up to 2 Years
Do
• Breastfeed as often as the child wants
• Offer food from the family pot
• Give at least 3/4 cup serving* at a time of:
• Mashed roti/rice/bread mixed in thick dal with added ghee/oil or khichri with added oil/ghee.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
with added ghee/oil or khichri with added oil/ghee. • Add cooked vegetables also in the servings OR 
• Mashed roti (Wheat/Ragi/Makai/Jowar/Bajra) OR rice mixed in undiluted milk, OR
• Sevian/dalia/halwa/kheer prepared in milk, OR
• Any cereal porridge cooked in milk, OR
• Mashed boiled potatoes
• Offer banana/biscuit/cheeko/mango/papaya
• Give 3 to 4 meals each day. Offer 1 to 2 snacks between meals. • Continue to feed your child slowly, patiently.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
child slowly, patiently. Encourage your child to eat. Remember
•Sit by the side of child and help her/him to finish the serving
•Wash your child’s hands with soap and water every time before and after feeding
2 years and older
Do
• Give a variety of family foods to your child, including animal source foods and vitamin A-rich fruits and vegetables
• Give at least 1 full cup (250ml) at each meal
• Give 3 to 4 meals each day
• Give 1 or 2 nutritious food between meals, such as: Banana/cheeko/mango/papaya as snacks
Remember
• Ensure that the child finishes the serving
• Teach your child to wash his hands with soap and water every time before and after feeding
*A good daily diet should be adequate in quantity and include an energy-rich food (for example, thick cereal 
porridge with added oil/ghee); meat, fish, eggs, or pulses; and fruits and vegetables.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
fish, eggs, or pulses; and fruits and vegetables. Egg is a good snack where 
culturally acceptable. Annexure 8

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
55
Counselling on Life Style Modifications
1. Diet
• Increase intake of green leafy vegetables and locally available seasonal fruits (at least 400 gms per day). • Consume less salt (<5 gms per day). Preparations which are high in salt and need to be moderated. • Steamed and boiled food should be preferred over fried food. • Avoid eating fast/junk foods and aerated drinks
• Use different oils such as mustard oil, rice bran oil, ground nut oil and til oil etc.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
oil, ground nut oil and til oil etc. as cooking medium
• Ghee and butter should be consumed in moderation. Palm oil, Vanaspati and Margarine should be avoided. • Consumptions of millets and legumes should be encouraged. For non-vegetarians, consumption of egg, fish 
and chicken to be encouraged. Red meat should be avoided or be consumed infrequently in small quantities. 2.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
or be consumed infrequently in small quantities. 2. Physical Activity and Stress Management
• Regular physical exercise 3-5 times a week should be encouraged
• At least 30 minutes of physical exercises per day is recommended for health and wellbeing. Encourage 
outdoor activities like walking, cycling, swimming, gardening, various outdoor sports. • Yoga & meditation to be promoted for stress management.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
& meditation to be promoted for stress management. Note: Beneficiaries suffering from hypertension, diabetes, chest pain etc. should be advised to take up 
physical exercise as recommended by the attending physician
3.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
exercise as recommended by the attending physician 3. Weight Control
• Overweight or obesity is to be assessed by measuring body mass index (BMI), which is calculated as weight 
in kg/height in meter square
• For Indian population BMI 18.5 to 22.9 is normal, 23 to 24.9 is considered as overweight and BMI of >25 is 
considered as obesity 
• Waist circumference is also an important measurement of central obesity and it should be <90 cm for men 
and <80 cm for women
4.

title: NHM VHSND National Guidelines
content:
for men and <80 cm for women 4. Avoidance of Alcohol/Tobacco cessation
• Use of Alcohol is to be discouraged. To be avoided by everyone as far as possible
• All non-smokers should be appreciated for not indulging in smoking. • All smokers should be strongly encouraged to quit smoking
Annexure 9 
Counselling for Prevention of NCDs

content:

meal. One IFA tablet should be provided to each girl every week on the fixed day.

content:
each girl every week on the fixed day. Estimation of IFA and Albendazole Tablets   
IFA tablets for the year = (Number of adolescent girls registered with 
ICDS x 52 tablets) + (52 tablets/ year for each AWW + 52 tablets/ year for 
ASHA). An additional 20 % stock as buffer to be added.  
Albendazole tablets for the year = Number of adolescent girls registered 
with AWC x 2 tablets of Albendazole. An additional 10 % stock as buffer 
to be added.

title: NHM WIFS IFA Supplementation Guidelines
content:
4  Ensure direct consumption of IFA tablet by adolescent girls under supervision. Ensure provision of safe drinking water for IFA tablet consumption  IFA tablets are to be taken after meal (after half hour to one hour gap) . AWW will orient girls on how to fill Individual Compliance cards. AWW to consume IFA tablets themselves  If the girl misses out on the consumption of the IFA tablet, ASHA should be asked to 
track the girl and ensure IFA tablet consumption.

title: NHM WIFS IFA Supplementation Guidelines
content:
track the girl and ensure IFA tablet consumption. The next week tablet will be given on 
fixed day only. AWW will screen adolescent girls for presence of moderate/severe anaemia by 
assessing nail bed, palmar, conjunctival and tongue pallor.

title: NHM WIFS IFA Supplementation Guidelines
content:
assessing nail bed, palmar, conjunctival and tongue pallor. ( Continue weekly IFA 
supplementation in an adolescent with significant anaemia till the time she is tested for 
haemoglobin level in health facility and started on treatment of anaemia)  Girls with significant pallor should be referred to appropriate nearby health facility for 
management of anaemia.

title: NHM WIFS IFA Supplementation Guidelines
content:
appropriate nearby health facility for management of anaemia. Minor side effects such as black stools, nausea and vomiting can occur in few cases, but 
side effects are often transitory and the frequency of side-effects of WIFS is much lower 
than with daily doses. Also side effects decrease over time  In case a girl complains of uneasiness /any side effects, the AWW will refer her to the 
ANM.

title: NHM WIFS IFA Supplementation Guidelines
content:
the AWW will refer her to the ANM. AWW should inform girls about benefits of IFA supplementation such as:  NOTE: Pregnant adolescent girls should be referred to nearest health facility and given 
IFA tablets as per ANC guidelines.

title: NHM WIFS IFA Supplementation Guidelines
content:
and given IFA tablets as per ANC guidelines.  Improved concentration in school, and 
school performance 
 Feeling stronger and less tired,   Increased energy levels and output in day to 
day work,   Increased appetite, 
 Improved overall capacity to work and earn 
  Better sleep 
  Improved skin appearance,   Regularization of menstruation 
 Building pre-pregnancy health

by state and the second dose should be given by February /six months after the first

the monthly report will be shared with ANM. AWW will maintain a supply-compliance register  KEY ACTIVITIES   10-19 years out-of-school girls to be given IFA and Albendazole

12  SAMPLE ADOLESCENT BALANCED DIET  10 – 12 years Adolescents Boys 
Early morning : 1 glass milk (200 ml) + 1 – 2 tsp sugar  Breakfast: 2 vegetable stuffed parantha (any) /2 -3 dosas stuffed with potato vegetable / 2 
bread slices with butter / 4 idlis with coconut chutney/ 1 cup vegetable upma + 1 cup tea 
(with 1 – 2 tsp sugar)  Mid-morning : 1 fruit like banana, apple, guava etc or 1 glass lemon juice (with 1 – 2 tsp sugar)  Lunch : 4 rotis or 2 katori cooked rice  or  2 rotis and 1 katori rice 
+ 1 katori green leafy vegetables (cooked in 1 tsp oil)  + 1 katori cooked dal or sprouts  (cooked 
in 1 tsp oil)  + 1 katori dahi (200 ml) like aloo raita or carrot raita etc  Tea : 1 cup tea (with 1 – 2 tsp sugar) + 1 rusk or 1 biscuit or aloo chaat  Dinner: 4 rotis or 2 katori rice  or 2 roti and 1 katori rice 
+ 1 katori cooked dal/pulses vegetables (cooked in 1 tsp oil)  + 1 katori any vegetable (cooked 
in 1 tsp oil)  10 – 12 years Adolescent girl  Menu  Early morning : 1 glass milk (200 ml) + 1 – 2 tsp sugar  Breakfast: 2 vegetable stuffed parantha (any) /2 -3 dosas stuffed with potato vegetable / 2 
bread slices with butter / 4 idlis with coconut chutney/ 1 cup vegetable upma + 1 cup tea (with 
1 – 2 tsp sugar)  Mid-morning : 1 fruit like banana, apple, guava etc  Lunch : 3 rotis or 1 ½ katori cooked rice or 2 roti and ½ katori rice  + 1 katori any green leafy vegetables (cooked in 1 tsp oil)  + 1 katori any cooked dal or sprouts  (cooked in 1 tsp oil)  + 1 katori dahi (200 ml) like aloo raita or carrot raita etc  Tea : 1 cup tea (with 1 – 2 tsp sugar) + 1 rusk or 1 biscuit  or aloo chat  Dinner: 3 rotis or 1 ½ katori rice  or 1 roti and ½ katori rice 
+ 1 katori any cooked dal/pulses vegetables (cooked in 1 tsp oil)  + 1 katori any vegetable 
(cooked in 1 tsp oil)

title: NIN RDA Brief Note 2020
content:
3  SFAs, MUFAs besides obtaining them from the diet, while the PUFAs, namely, linoleic acid (also 
known as n-6 or omega 6 or LA) and alpha linolenic (also known as n-3 or omega 3 or ALA) cannot 
be synthesized. Hence, n-6 and n-3 are termed essential fatty acids (EFAs). Our body depends on 
dietary sources for n-3 and n-6 FA as they are not made in our body.

title: NIN RDA Brief Note 2020
content:
as they are not made in our body. Hence, one should take adequate 
amounts of nuts, oilseeds along with recommended levels of diverse foods (My Plate for the Day), 
to meet the required amounts of 6.6 grams of n-6 PUFA and 2.2 grams of n-3 PUFA for all the 
essential functions in the body.

title: NIN RDA Brief Note 2020
content:
for all the essential functions in the body. The visible fat (cooking oil) must be limited to 20-50g (4 to 10 
teaspoon full) per person/ day depending on the level of energy (calorie) requirement, which is based 
on physical activity and physiological status. (For example, not more than 27g visible fat/ cooking 
oil is recommended for a 2000 kcal diet/ day).

title: NIN RDA Brief Note 2020
content:
is recommended for a 2000 kcal diet/ day). Recommendation on vitamins and mineral requirement 
For vitamins and minerals, the EAR and RDA are determined among normal individuals by using 
approaches such as (1) Factorial: calculate losses in feces, urine, sweat and estimate the 
bioavailability, then add the requirement for growth in children or milk output in lactating women 
(2) balance studies: minimum required to maintain homeostasis; intake – losses = 0, (3) or calculate 
from maintenance of optimal function, such as BP and sodium, calcium and bone density etc.

title: NIN RDA Brief Note 2020
content:
BP and sodium, calcium and bone density etc. The 
present committee has done extensive deliberations on recommendations for minerals like iron, 
calcium, phosphorus, zinc, selenium and iodine, and vitamins such as B vitamins and vitamin A. Data available on dietary intakes, balance studies, fecal loss- urinary loss in different geographical 
states, absorption studies, turnover studies were considered.

title: NIN RDA Brief Note 2020
content:
geographical states, absorption studies, turnover studies were considered. For some nutrients (B1, B2, B6) enzyme 
activity coefficient studies were considered. As can be seen from the Tables 3 (males) and 4 (females) 
the RDA for all the vitamins and minerals are higher in the ICMR-NIN 2020 recommendation 
compared to the 2010.

title: NIN RDA Brief Note 2020
content:
the ICMR-NIN 2020 recommendation compared to the 2010. However, as the distribution of nutrient requirement and dietary intake is 
expected to superimpose among healthy population groups, considering RDA as the reference the 
distribution shifts to the right with RDA as the mean and the entire population will shift beyond and 
above the requirements and a significant proportion may be at TUL.

title: NIN RDA Brief Note 2020
content:
and a significant proportion may be at TUL. Hence, the present committee 
recommended EAR for population requirement/ adequacy estimations, menu or diet planning for 
individuals, which is in alignment with global recommendation.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
3.3.2 To reduce sugar and salt content in supplementary nutrition provided at Anganwadi
centers, a multi-pronged approach is needed, focusing on using less sugar and salt in recipes,
incorporating naturally sweet and savoury ingredients and promoting healthy eating habits
through education. A. Take Home Ration (THR): Recommendations for reducing added sugars and salt in
the THR are as under:
. Limit Added Sugar: Refined sugar should not be used.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
Added Sugar: Refined sugar should not be used. Jaggery may be used for
sweetening, only if needed. Eliminate or minimize the use of jaggery in recipes to
less than 5% of the total energy. . Added sugar should not be used for children under 2 years of age. . Restrict Discretionary Calories: Avoid using sugary drinks, sweets, and other
high-sugar foods as part of the supplementary nutrition. . Minimize Added Salt: Reduce the amount of salt used in cooking to bare
minimum.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
of salt used in cooking to bare minimum. lnstead, boost the natural flavour of food with herbs, spices, and other
natural seasonings. . Design THR recipes without adding salt and/or sugar so that the beneficiaries may
use the same as per their taste preference, if needed. B. Hot Cooked Meals & Morning Snacks: To reduce sugar content in Hot Cooked Meals
& Moming Snacks at Anganwadi centers, focus on limiting added sugars.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
at Anganwadi centers, focus on limiting added sugars. This can be
achieved by reducing or eliminating sugar and/or salt in recipes, using fruits &
vegetables as a natural sweetening agent. . Added sugar should not be used for children under 2 years of age. . Review Recipes: Examine the recipes to identify where sugar is added, such as
in sweet dalia/rice, halwa, desserts, etc. Refined sugar should not be used. Jaggery may be used for sweetening, only if needed.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
may be used for sweetening, only if needed. Eliminate or minimize the use
of jaggery in recipes to less than 5olo ofthe total energy. lf eliminating sugar/jaggery
entirely is not feasible, try reducing the amount of sugar used in the recipe by a
small percentage, gradually decreasing it over time as taste preferences adjust'
.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
it over time as taste preferences adjust' . Replace with Natural Sweeteners: Consider using fruits and vegetables with
naturally sweet taste like dates, banana, mango, papaya, carrots, sweet potatoes,
etc. These add sweetness while also providing fiber and other essential nutrients. . Reduce the number of sweet recipes served under Morning Snacks and Hot
Cooked Meals. . Minimize Added Salt: Reduce the amount of salt used in cooking to bare
minimum.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
of salt used in cooking to bare minimum. lnstead, boost the natural flavour of food with herbs, spices' and other
natural seasonings. 3.4 Use of foods high in fats, salt and sugar (HFSS) and other ready-to-eat fast foods'
bakery foods and processed foods which may contain trans-fats and saturated fats must be
avoided (example: biscuits, chips, sugary drinks, etc.).

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
be avoided (example: biscuits, chips, sugary drinks, etc.). 3.5 Adherence to Food Safety and Standards Regulations: lt should be ensured that
the ingredients used for production of supplementary nutrition are safe and comply with all the
applicible provisions of Food Safety and Standards Regulations, 2011 and any further
amendments to the regulations.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
3.6 Preservatives, colours, flavours and other synthetic ingredients: The foods for
infant nutrition (foods intended for children up to the age of 2 years) should also be free from
preservatives, added colours, flavours and other synthetic ingredients and should comply with
the provisions of Food Safety and Standards (Foods for lnfant Nutrition) Regulations, 2020.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
and Standards (Foods for lnfant Nutrition) Regulations, 2020. 3.7 Emulsifiers: Only emulsifiers which are permitted under the Food Safety and
Standards (Foods for lnfant Nutrition) Regulalions, 2020 and any further amendment to the
regulation should be used. 3.8 Artificial Sweeteners/sugar substitutes: Do not use any artificial sweeteners or
sugar substitutes under supplementary nutrition for pregnant and lactating women and
children. 4.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
for pregnant and lactating women and children. 4. Reduce use of fats/oils in the diet
4.1 lntake of oils and fats should be in moderation. Cooking methods such as saut6ing,
steaming, grilling, boiling or baking should be used instead of frying. 4.2 The intake of cooking oil/visible fat should not be more than 27-309 lday (adults).

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
should not be more than 27-309 lday (adults). The
requirement of fat, however, depends on the level of energy (calorie) requirement, which is
based on physical activity and physiological status. It is recommended that adult women or
men with sedentary lifestyle limit daily fat intake to 20 g and 30 g of visible fat, respectively.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
g and 30 g of visible fat, respectively. Adult men with moderate and heavy physical activity may consume visible fat up to 40 g and
50 g respectively, while adult women with moderate and heavy physical activity may consume
up to 30 g and 40 g respectively. 4.3 To enhance nutrition and flavour, oilseeds/nuts paste can be used in ptace of refined
oils/fats.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
can be used in ptace of refined oils/fats. 4.4 High intake of saturated fats (SF) enhances calorie intake and increases the risk of
heart diseases and stroke, hence must be limited to not more than 10% of total energy intake. Saturated fatty acids are found in fatty meat, dairy foods, and hard fats and oils such as butter,
ghee, lard, palm/palmolein oil, coconut oil and any product made using these sources of fats. 4.5 Trans-fats are harmful and must be avoided.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
4.5 Trans-fats are harmful and must be avoided. Trans-fats are produced during
hydrogenation of vegetable cooking oils (example, vanaspati). Trans-fat consumption
increases risk of heart disease, stroke, diabetes, breast cancer, colon cancer, preeclampsia,
disorders of nervous system and vision in infants. Trans-fats are found in vanaspati, fried
foods, margarine, ready-to-eat fast foods, bakery foods and processed foods.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
ready-to-eat fast foods, bakery foods and processed foods. Use of
vanaspati as a cooking medium should be avoided as it contains trans-fats and saturated fats. 4.6 Repeated heating of cooking oils generates harmful oxidative compounds which may
increase the risk of cardiovascular diseases and cancer and hence must be avoided.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
diseases and cancer and hence must be avoided. At
household level, edible oil once used for frying, should be filtered and may be used for curry
preparation but using the same oil for frying again should be avoided. Also, such oils should
be consumed in a day or two. Storing 'used'oils for a long time should be avoided, as the rate
of deterioration is high in such oils.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
rate of deterioration is high in such oils. 4.7 Minimize consumption of ready-to-eat fast foods, bakery foods and processed foods
which may contain lrans-fats and saturated fats. 5. Healthy Lifestyle and dietary practices
5.1 Good nutrition is vital at each stage of the human lifecycle. Nutrients that we obtain
through food have vital effects on physical growth and development, maintenance of normal
body function, physical activity and health.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
of normal body function, physical activity and health. The quantities of foods needed to meet the nutrient
requirements vary with age, gender, physiological status and physical activity. Dietary intakes

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
lower or higher than the body's requirements can lead to undernutrition or overnutrition
respectively. Eating too little food during certain significant periods of life such as infancy,
childhood, adolescence, pregnancy and lactation and eating too much at any age can lead to
harmful consequences. Appropriate dietary habits and physical activity through all the stages
of one's life are essential for the maintenance of holistic health.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
are essential for the maintenance of holistic health. 5.2 Therefore, efforts should be made to promote healthy lifestyle and dietary practices in
line with the Dietary Guidelines for lndians, 2024, released by the ICMR-National lnstitute of
Nutrition. The guidelines can be 
accessed at 
the 
link:  nin. res. in/dietarvquidelines/pdfis/locale/DG107052024P. pdf. A summary of
recommendations is at Annexure-lV.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
pdf. A summary of recommendations is at Annexure-lV. 8
o
States/ UTs are requested to adopt these guidelines as soon as possible

6. Nutrition Education: ln addition to the above-mentioned strategies to achieve healthy
dietary and lifestyle patterns, it is important to conduct targeted awareness
programmes/activities for officers, field functionaries and community at large. .

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
officers, field functionaries and community at large. . Educate Anganwadi workers to conduct regular awareness sessions for mothers and
caregivers on the importance of adopting healthy diet and lifestyle, specifically
reducing sugar, salt and oil intake, consumption of a wide variety of nutritious foods,
including fruits, vegetables, and whole grains, to ensure a balanced diet. .

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
whole grains, to ensure a balanced diet. . Engage the local community in promoting healthy eating habits and supporting
Anganwadi centers in providing nutritious meals. 7. Monitoring and Evaluation:
. Regular Quality Checks: lmplement regular monitoring and quality checks of the
supplementary nutrition provided at Anganwadi centers to ensure compliance with the
recommended guidelines. .

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
to ensure compliance with the recommended guidelines. . Nutritional Assessment: Conduct regular nutritional assessments of children and
women to track their health and nutritional status. (Dr. Monika Singh)

It is suggested to include fresh whole fruits and fresh milk/WMP to the extent possible to improve bioavailable nutrients.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
Annexure-III  Guidance on Use of Fortified Foods under Supplementary Nutrition 
• The suggested food baskets in Annexure-II are for provision of Supplementary Nutrition to 
the beneficiaries of Anganwadi Services in order to meet the nutrition norms given in the 
revised Schedule-II of the National Food Security Act.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
revised Schedule-II of the National Food Security Act. • The food baskets have been calculated to provide the recommended levels of energy, 
protein and micronutrients (calcium, zinc, iron, folate, vitamin B6, vitamin A and B12). None 
of the micronutrients are needed to be supplemented separately.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
the micronutrients are needed to be supplemented separately. However, if any fortified 
food item is used for preparation of supplementary nutrition, then the additional amounts of 
micronutrients provided by that food may be calculated as under: 
• If Double Fortified Salt is used, it will contribute additional iron @ 1mg/g of salt.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
will contribute additional iron @ 1mg/g of salt. • If Fortified Rice or Fortified Wheat Flour is used, it will contribute additional iron @ 
0.028-0.043 mg/g, additional folic acid @ 0.075-0.125 microgram/g and additional 
vitamin B12 @ 0.00075-0.00125 microgram/g of rice or wheat flour. • If Fortified oil is used, it will contribute additional vitamin A @ 6.0-9.9 microgram/g 
and additional vitamin D @ 0.11-0.16 microgram/g (or 4.4-6.4 IU/g) of oil.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
@ 0.11-0.16 microgram/g (or 4.4-6.4 IU/g) of oil. • If Fortified Milk is used, it will contribute additional vitamin A @ 0.27-0.45 
microgram/ml and additional vitamin D @ 0.005-0.008 microgram/ml (or 0.20-0.32 
IU/ml) of milk. • Under no circumstances should the level of micronutrients go beyond the respective 
Tolerable Upper Limits (Appendix-I) if sourced from multiple sources including natural 
foods, fortified foods, nutritional and health supplements.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
natural foods, fortified foods, nutritional and health supplements. (Ref: Technical report of NIN: Revision of Food and Nutrition norms under Schedule II of the

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
Annexure-IV 
Summary of Important Recommendations from Dietary Guidelines for Indians, 2024  A. Balanced and Nutritious Dietary practices: A balanced diet is a wholesome and 
nutritionally adequate diet. It provides a variety of nutrients that perform a wide range of 
functions in the body. • 
Include a variety of foods in the diet since there is no single food item with all the 
essential nutrients.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
single food item with all the essential nutrients. The ICMR-NIN, 'My Plate for the Day' recommends a minimum of 
eight food groups, with vegetables, fruits, green leafy vegetables, roots and tubers 
forming essentially half the plate of the recommended foods per day. The other major 
portion is occupied by cereals and millets, followed by pulses, flesh foods, eggs, nuts, 
oilseeds and milk/curd.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
pulses, flesh foods, eggs, nuts, oilseeds and milk/curd. • 
Consume at least 50% of cereals and other grains as whole grains for adequate 
nutrients and fibre. • 
All cereal/millet-based diets are to be accompanied with adequate pulses or beans for 
good quality protein and fibre. The ideal Cereal:Pulse ratio is 2:1 to 3:1. • 
Avoid ultra-processed foods (UPFs) and foods high in fat, sugar and salt (HFSS) and 
choose healthy options (Appendix-II).

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
and salt (HFSS) and choose healthy options (Appendix-II). • 
Avoid sugar, if possible, or restrict to 20 g to 25 g per day (adults). Avoid food and 
beverages with added sugars. • 
Include traditional and seasonal local foods in the diet. • 
Drink adequate quantities of water to meet the daily fluid requirements. Prefer 
buttermilk, tender coconut water, lemon water, etc., as beverages in hot weather 
instead of synthetic soft drinks and carbonated beverages.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
instead of synthetic soft drinks and carbonated beverages. • 
Exposure to sunlight for obtaining vitamin D is also recommended. Exposure to 
sunlight for about 30 minutes preferably between 11.00 am to 2.00 pm maintains 
vitamin D status which helps in calcium absorption. B. Adopt a healthy lifestyle to prevent overweight and obesity 
• 
Prevention of overweight/obesity is important.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
and obesity • Prevention of overweight/obesity is important. There is a tendency to gain weight 
mostly around 20 years of age; and among women after childbirth because of less 
physical activity and higher calorie intake. Obesity is also rapidly increasing among 
children and adolescence. Therefore, caution must be exercised during these periods. • 
Maintain appropriate body mass index (BMI) or weight-for-height. • 
Regular yoga and physical activity are essential.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
• Regular yoga and physical activity are essential. Exercise intensity and duration should 
be increased gradually over a period of time. Shortness of breath, pain, nausea, 
vomiting, and headache are warning signs that the body needs rest. o Adults: At least 30–60 minutes of moderate activity most days. Activities include 
walking, yoga, cycling, dancing, or sports. o Children: At least 60 minutes of moderate-to-vigorous intensity activity or play 
daily.

title: Nutrition Norms Supplementary Sept2025
content:
minutes of moderate-to-vigorous intensity activity or play daily. • 
If overweight/obese, slow and steady reduction in body weight is advisable. Extreme 
approaches for weight loss may lead to health hazards. A reduction of half a kilogram

1.1. Background of Poshan 2.0
1.2. Rationale for Poshan 2.0
The  (MoWCD) strives to ensure well-
nourished and happy children as well as confident and self-reliant women, by
providing them with an environment that is accessible, affordable, reliable, and free
from all forms of discrimination and violence.

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
free from all forms of discrimination and violence. The prime objective of the Ministry is to
address gaps in State action for women and children and to promote inter-ministerial
and inter-sectoral convergence to create gender-equitable and child-centered
legislation, policies, and programmes.

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
create gender-equitable and child-centered legislation, policies, and programmes. In order to achieve this, the Cabinet approved three important Umbrella Schemes of

Shakti, and Mission Vatsalya.

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
Mission Poshan 2.0, Mission Shakti, and Mission Vatsalya. These are strategic interventions for addressing
malnutrition concerns and for the empowerment, development, and protection of
women and children
Mission POSHAN 2.0 along with Saksham Anganwadi is an Integrated Nutrition
Support Programme (hereinafter referred to as ‘Poshan 2.0’), which seeks to address
the challenges of malnutrition among children, adolescent girls, pregnant women,
and lactating mothers.

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022

children, adolescent girls, pregnant women, and lactating mothers. The focus of Poshan 2.0 is on maternal nutrition, Infant and
Young Child Feeding (IYCF) norms, treatment of Moderate Acute Malnutrition (MAM)
/ Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM), and wellness through Ayurveda, Yoga and
Naturopathy, Unani, Siddha, and Homeopathy (AYUSH).

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
Yoga and Naturopathy, Unani, Siddha, and Homeopathy (AYUSH). As per the MoWCD, nutrition policies have remained consistent over the years and
have been slow to transition towards a more integrated approach to nutrition security. Past interventions in terms of both nutrition quality and delivery have been inadequate.

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022

both nutrition quality and delivery have been inadequate. The Anganwadi Services under Integrated Child Development Services (ICDS) have
focused on ensuring calorie sufficiency, neglecting quality and diversity of diets and
behavioural change towards better nutrition.

contributes to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs),

Education.

as a result of which, the current program has largely been driven by calorie intake rather

Take-Home Ration (THR) and Hot Cooked Meals (HCM). Traditional wisdom in nutritional practices too has not

poor implementation with an absence of last-mile tracking. Poshan 2.0 is designed to address the existing challenges through transparency,

etc., converge on a household, for reducing undernutrition. MoWCD shall

build awareness and create Jan Andolan around nutrition. The District Magistrate (DM)/Collector, who is the nodal point in the district, shall chair,

●  Assist Primary Health Centre staff in implementing the health component of the  programme, and assist ANM in the administration of IFA and Vitamin A
●  Coordinate with other local institutions and involve lady school teachers and  primary/middle school girls for relevant activities
●  Guide ASHA engaged under the  in the delivery of healthcare  services and maintenance of records

●  Perform any bonafide task related to Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 as directed  by the Government of India depending on the circumstances
●  Cook and serve food to beneficiaries

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
in her absence and discharge her responsibilities 5. SUPPLEMENTARY NUTRITION PROGRAM
Supplementary Nutrition, one of the six components under Anganwadi Services, is
provided to beneficiaries of Anganwadi Services through a network of 14 lakh Anganwadi
Centres located across the country. It is primarily designed to bridge the gap between
Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) and the Average Daily Intake (ADI).

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
Allowance (RDA) and the Average Daily Intake (ADI). The
National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013 mandates provision of Supplementary Nutrition
to every pregnant woman and lactating mother up to 6 months after childbirth, and every
child in the age group of 6 months to 6 years (including those suffering from malnutrition).

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
to 6 years (including those suffering from malnutrition). Recipes for supplementary nutrition must conform to prescribed nutrition norms, and
locally available ingredients must be used in the recipes, considering local taste
preferences.

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
●  Supplementary Nutrition shall be provided at AWC to registered beneficiaries who visit  their respective AWCs during working hours
●  Supplementary Nutrition shall be provided at AWC to registered beneficiaries who visit  their respective AWCs during working hours
●  Only those beneficiaries who are registered at the AWC are entitled to receive  supplementary Nutrition
●  Pregnant women, lactating mothers and children up to the age of 6 years residing  even temporarily in the coverage area of the AWC shall be registered and provided  supplementary nutrition and other services 
●  In case the beneficiary is registered at another AWC, she/he shall be migrated to the  new AWC during the period of stay through the migration facility in the Poshan  Tracker
●  All pregnant women and lactating mothers, children in the age group of 6-36 months,  SAM children and adolescent girls in the age group of 14-18 years (wherever  applicable) are entitled to receive THR and children in the age group of 3-6 years are  entitled to receive HCM and Morning Snacks under the Anganwadi Services from the  AWC where they are registered
●  At the time of registration, the beneficiary should be informed about their entitlement  of Supplementary Nutrition, periodicity of distribution of THR, and time of provision of  HCM at the AWC
●  The aim of having diversity in the diet is to provide quality protein and essential  micronutrients
●  Serving of fresh and locally produced green vegetables, fruits, medicinal plants and  herbs, fortified rice, and millets should be encouraged to beneficiaries under the  scheme to promote health, wellness, and immunity and to manage anemia
●  Use of nuts and oilseeds like groundnut and sesame seeds (they provide energy,  protein as well as fats and are a good source of nutrients like calcium, phosphorus,  magnesium and some B-Complex vitamins like folic acid, etc.) 
●  Only jaggery should be used for sweetening (white sugar should not be used)
●  Food items for THR (not raw ration) and HCM are specific to the States and shall  include locally grown/available wholesome fruits and vegetables
●  Food ingredients used in Supplementary Nutrition shall comprise of cereals, pulses,  millets (coarse grains), nuts, and some fat to increase the energy content
●  Millets should be mandatorily supplied at least once a week and suitably integrated in  THR and HCM in a palatable form

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
●  Supplementary nutrition is provided to beneficiaries in two servings - Morning Snacks  and HCMs
●  HCMs are the prime focus of nutrition support in order to encourage local dietary  diversity and intake of fresh meals
●  THR must ensure dietary diversity and last-mile delivery to the beneficiary and must  be prepared using locally available and culturally appropriate food ingredients 
●  THR should not be given in the form of raw ration (i.e., wheat, dal, rice, etc.)
●  While HCMs can be given in lieu of THR, SAM children should be given additional  nutrition in the form of THR
●  THR products that are being delivered to target beneficiaries should be clearly  delineated based on the nutritional requirements of the beneficiaries (children who  are SAM, children between 6 months to 3 years of age, adolescent girls aged 14-18  years, pregnant women and lactating mothers)
●  Government websites should have updated and detailed information on THR product  (including calories, proteins, micro-nutrients, energy, etc.), formats, production models  and nutritional profiling across beneficiary groups
●  THR is distributed regularly twice a month to registered beneficiaries 
●  THR is properly tracked to last mile beneficiary and nutritional standards are  monitored to ensure quantity and quality of the meal
●  State/UT must introduce transparent processes for procurement of Supplementary  Nutrition as per General Financial Rules (GFR) and vigilance guidelines, and ensure  that the THR procured conforms to technical and nutritional standards set by MoWCD
●  Proper receipts and registers for food grains (wheat, fortified rice, millets, coarse  grains, dal and other edibles etc.) supplied to the AWC should be maintained by the  AWW 
●  Monthly oversight services and audits shall be conducted by Village Health Sanitation  and Nutrition Committee (VHSNC), Lady Supervisor, and CDPO 
●  Details of food grains allocation and utilisation should be monitored under Poshan  Tracker

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022

should be done through FSSAI-owned or authorized laboratories. State Governments and the Union Territory Administrations will develop a testing protocol

at the AWC or at the Block level. CDPO or Supervisor

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022

of THR and HCM in randomly selected AWCs. State Governments / UT Administrations must ensure the following to develop community

PoshanVatikas (also known as nutri-gardens) are community or kitchen gardens set up
for the cultivation of fresh food products for local consumption by community members.

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
food products for local consumption by community members. Poshan Vatikas are being set up with the objective of addressing dietary diversity, food
security, and healthy eating practices to combat malnutrition among the beneficiaries,
and to promote Government of India’s ‘Aatma Nirbhar Bharat’ and ‘Vocal for Local’
campaigns.

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
‘Aatma Nirbhar Bharat’ and ‘Vocal for Local’ campaigns. As  per the Poshan 2.0 guidelines, Poshan Vatikas shall be set up at AWCs, government-
led schools, gram panchayat lands, or any other vacant land in the village. The Poshan
Vatikas can also be retrofitted with poultry and fishing units in the local areas, where
culturally acceptable.

title: Poshan2 Mini Course Guidelines 2022
content:
units in the local areas, where culturally acceptable. The District Nutrition Committee headed by the District Collector shall oversee the
development of Poshan Vatikas, in convergence with the Department of Forest,
Agriculture, Horticulture, PRIs, Rural Development, and AYUSH.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 
3  Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 (hereinafter referred to as Poshan 2.0) is an 
Integrated Nutrition Support Programme. It seeks to address the challenges of 
malnutrition in children, adolescent girls, pregnant women and lactating mothers through 
a strategic shift in nutrition content and delivery and by creation of a convergent eco- 
system to develop and promote practices that nurture health, wellness and immunity.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
promote practices that nurture health, wellness and immunity. Poshan 2.0 shall focus on Maternal Nutrition, Infant and Young Child Feeding Norms, 
Treatment of MAM/SAM and Wellness through AYUSH. It will rest on the pillars of 
Convergence, Governance, and Capacity-building. Poshan Abhiyan will be the pillar for 
Outreach and will cover innovations related to nutritional support, ICT interventions, 
Media Advocacy and Research, Community Outreach and Jan Andolan.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Education is an integral component of the programme. Recognizing that early childhood care constitutes the foundation of human development, 
the Scheme is designed to promote holistic development of children under six years of 
age through improved ECCE content and delivery of cognitive, emotional, social and 
intellectual development of the child to make all pre-schooler’s school ready and for 
seamless integration of children in the age group of 5-6 in Grade I under the National 
Education Policy, 2020.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Grade I under the National Education Policy, 2020. The programme is specifically designed to reach disadvantaged 
and low-income groups, for effective disparity reduction. The current Anganwadi Services Scheme is one of the largest and unique programmes 
of the Govt. of India for early childhood care and development.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
of India for early childhood care and development. It is a firm testament of 
the country’s commitment to its children and nursing mothers to respond to the challenge 
of providing pre-school non-formal education on one hand and break the vicious cycle of 
malnutrition, morbidity, reduced learning capacity and mortality on the other.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
reduced learning capacity and mortality on the other. The 
beneficiaries under this scheme are children in the age group of 0-6 years, pregnant 
women and lactating mothers and adolescent girls in the age group 14-18 years. The rationale for Mission Poshan 2.0 arises from the challenges plaguing the extant 
supplementary nutrition programme.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
the challenges plaguing the extant supplementary nutrition programme. Nutrition policies have remained consistent over the 
years and have been slow to transition towards a more integrated approach to nutrition 
security. Past interventions in terms of both nutrition quality and delivery have been 
inadequate. The Anganwadi Services under ICDS have focused on ensuring calorie 
sufficiency, neglecting quality and diversity of diets and behavioural change towards

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 
4  better nutrition. Focus of the current program has largely been driven by calorie intake 
rather than on balanced diets with absence of micro-nutrients in Take-Home Ration (THR) 
and Hot Cooked Meals (HCM). Traditional wisdom in nutritional practices have not been 
leveraged.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
wisdom in nutritional practices have not been leveraged. Scheme execution and implementation have been affected by absence of 
effective participation of beneficiaries and local stakeholders pointing to weak community 
ownership or stake in local Anganwadi activities. A critical challenge faced is poor 
implementation with absence of last-mile tracking.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
is poor implementation with absence of last-mile tracking. Poshan 2.0 is designed to address the above challenges through transparency, 
accountability, balanced diets, diet diversity and quality, greater grassroots involvement 
and last-mile delivery of services supported by key strategies, viz., corrective strategies 
to address nutrition related deficiencies, nutrition awareness strategies to develop good 
eating habits for sustainable health and well-being, strategies for communication and 
development of green eco-systems such as Poshan Vatikas.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
development of green eco-systems such as Poshan Vatikas. 1.1 
Vision  Poshan 2.0 seeks to address the challenging situation of malnutrition among children 
up to the age of 6 years, adolescent girls (14-18 years) and pregnant and lactating 
women. Poshan 2.0 is crucial for India’s development as women and children comprise over two- 
thirds of India’s population. The achievement of the Sustainable Development Goals 
lies at the fore-front of this programme design.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
lies at the fore-front of this programme design. Poshan 2.0 shall contribute to the SDGs, 
specifically, SDG 2 on Zero Hunger and SDG 4 on Quality Education. The Mission will focus on the fundamental importance of nutrition and early childhood 
care and education for the well-being, growth and development of children into healthy 
and productive adults.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
development of children into healthy and productive adults. 1.2 
Objectives  To prepare a comprehensive strategy to address the challenge of malnutrition, the 
Supplementary Nutrition Programme under Anganwadi Services, Scheme for Adolescent 
Girls and Poshan Abhiyaan have been aligned under Poshan 2.0 as an Integrated 
Nutrition Support Programme.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 
5  Poshan 2.0 shall focus on Maternal Nutrition, Infant and Young Child Feeding Norms, 
Treatment Protocols for SAM/MAM and Wellness through AYUSH practices to 
reduce wasting and under-weight prevalence besides stunting and anemia, 
supported by the ‘Poshan Tracker’, a new, robust ICT centralised data system which 
is being linked with the RCH Portal (Anmol) of MoHFW.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
linked with the RCH Portal (Anmol) of MoHFW. Poshan 2.0 shall seek to optimize the quality and delivery of nutrition under the 
Supplementary Nutrition Program.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
delivery of nutrition under the Supplementary Nutrition Program. 1.3 
Important components of Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0  With a view to address various gaps and shortcomings in the on-going nutrition 
programme and to improve implementation as well as to accelerate improvement in 
nutrition and child development outcomes, the existing scheme components have 
been re-organized under Poshan 2.0 into the primary verticals given below:   Nutrition Support for POSHAN through Supplementary Nutrition Programme 
(SNP) for children of the age group of 06 months to 6 years, pregnant women 
and lactating mothers (PWLM); and for Adolescent Girls in the age group of 
14 to 18 years in Aspirational Districts and North Eastern Region (NER); 
 Early Childhood Care and Education [3-6 years]and early stimulation for (0-3 
years); 
 Anganwadi Infrastructure including modern, upgraded Saksham Anganwadi; 
and 
 Poshan Abhiyaan  These guidelines are being issued under the provisions of Sections 4(a), 5(1) (a), 6 
& 7 of National Food Security Act, 2013 and shall come into force with immediate 
effect.

and shall come into force with immediate effect. The Streamlined Guidelines issued on 13.01.2021 (hereinafter called ‘Streamlined

be involved in implementing the Scheme, as well as State, district and grassroots 
level functionaries.

well as State, district and grassroots level functionaries. It is intended to serve as a reference manual for officials at the

Scheme and processes for implementation and highlight the roles 
and 
responsibilities of various line departments as part of convergence.

Roles and responsibilities of State Administration  Nutrition requires convergence of various services that contribute to improved 
nutritional status.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024

various services that contribute to improved nutritional status. This requires strong and effective multi-department convergent 
efforts and actions to address malnutrition.

multi-department convergent efforts and actions to address malnutrition. Therefore, Chief Secretary of the 
States/UTs shall coordinate the activities of various departments through a State

regarding Nutritional Indicators on regular basis.

progress made regarding Nutritional Indicators on regular basis. Secretaries of different departments may assess how their schemes/programs can

Roles and responsibilities of District Administration  The District Magistrate shall be the Nodal Point in the district for monitoring nutritional 
status and quality standards.

district for monitoring nutritional status and quality standards. The DM/Collector shall chair, supervise and monitor the

9 have to be mandatorily certified nutrition experts. The Child Development Project

Indicator of the DM/Collector for nutritional improvement of beneficiaries, 
especially SAM/MAM children; 
 undertake overall administration and coordination of the nutrition project and 
ensure smooth and effective delivery of all intended services in the project 
jurisdiction; 
 conduct periodic monitoring, including surprise spot-checks, collection of 
samples for quality testing of supplementary nutrition (THR and HCM) 
provided, ensuring adequate measures for food safety and hygiene are 
followed throughout the supply chain, assessing the quality of pre-school 
delivery etc., to ensure quality and undertake necessary course correction; 
 monitor distribution of necessary stocks for delivery of key services; 
 participate in the VHSND meetings, community-based events, Jan Andolan 
activities etc.

families at least once in a year. f. To organize non-formal pre-school activities in the Anganwadi for children in the

women to adopt family planning/birth control measures. i. To help pregnant and lactating mothers visiting the Anganwadi centre to get the

Poshan Tracker  IT systems are being leveraged to strengthen and bring about transparency in nutrition 
delivery support systems. The ‘Poshan Tracker’ application was rolled out by MoWCD on 
1st March 2021 through National e-Governance Division (NeGD), MyGov as an important 
governance tool.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Division (NeGD), MyGov as an important governance tool. Technology under the Poshan Tracker is being leveraged for dynamic 
identification of stunting, wasting, under-weight prevalence among children and last mile 
tracking of nutrition service delivery. The POSHAN Tracker will enable real-time monitoring and tracking of all AWCs, AWWs and 
beneficiaries on defined indicators. Beneficiaries will be Aadhaar seeded to ensure last mile 
tracking and delivery of services.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024

ensure last mile tracking and delivery of services. Poshan Tracker will help Mission Poshan 2.0 in generating 
data, providing feedback to Program Managers and documenting the impact of scheme on

interventions based on the data from the Poshan Tracker, thereby facilitating continuous 
evaluation and the progress of different components.

continuous evaluation and the progress of different components. 2.7

14  3.1 
Package of Services  The Anganwadi Services Scheme provides the following package of services in an integrated 
manner to eligible beneficiaries:  i. supplementary nutrition 
ii. pre-school non-formal education 
iii. nutrition & health education 
iv. immunization 
v. health check-up and 
vi.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
education iv. immunization v. health check-up and vi. referral services  Three of the six services, viz., immunization, health check-up and referral services, are related 
to health and are provided by through NHM & Public Health Infrastructure. 3.1 
Salient Features  The Anganwadi Services Scheme under Poshan 2.0 has been approved for implementation 
during the 15th Finance Commission period i.e. from 2021-22 to 2025-26.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Finance Commission period i.e. from 2021-22 to 2025-26. The salient features 
of the scheme are as follows:  i. Beneficiaries covered under the Scheme are ‘Children upto the age of 6 years; 
Pregnant Women and Lactating Mothers (PW&LM) and Adolescent Girls (14-18 years) 
in Aspirational Districts and North Eastern States. ii. This Scheme is open to all eligible beneficiaries on demand, irrespective of caste, 
religion and income criteria.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
demand, irrespective of caste, religion and income criteria. The only pre-condition is that the beneficiary has to be 
registered at the nearest Anganwadi Centre with Aadhaar identification. A child's 
Aadhaar card shall not be mandatory for availing the benefits under the Scheme. The 
benefits under the scheme can be accessed using the mother's Aadhaar card. iii.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
be accessed using the mother's Aadhaar card. iii. Funds for Aadhaar enrolment kits have been placed with the States/UTs and effort 
shall be made by States/UTs to enroll beneficiaries at the AWC itself. iv. It shall be the duty and responsibility of AWW, Supervisor, Child Development Project 
Officer (CDPO) and District Programme Officer (DPO) concerned, to ensure that 
Supplementary Nutrition is provided to beneficiaries without any disruption. Assistance

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 
15  shall be extended by them to a beneficiary in obtaining Aadhaar Card, in case the 
beneficiary does not possess Aadhaar identification. v. Health check-up and referral services under Anganwadi Services shall continue to be 
provided through NHM and Public Health Infrastructure by ASHA and ANM. Besides, 
ASHA and ANM shall undertake regular screening of beneficiaries for assessment of 
anemia and diabetes.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024

of beneficiaries for assessment of anemia and diabetes. ASHA and ANM shall additionally screen children with special 
needs who require assistance for disabilities that may be medical or psychological. vi.

 IFA tablets during pregnancy and post-pregnancy 
 12 months to 5-year-old child: De-worming tablet once in 6 months 
 Pregnant Women: 1 de-worming tablet during second trimester 
 Vitamin A supplementation for 9 months to 5-year-old child bi-annually 
 Diarrhoea management: ORS, Zinc Supplementation 
Referral Services: 
 Severely Malnourished (SAM) to be referred to hospital or Nutrition Rehabilitation 
Centre 
 Care of Sick children

Health schedule  vii.

100% measurement of the children.

be made for 100% measurement of the children. Any child who could not be measured in a

Anganwadi Workers and Helpers: Qualification and Benefits under the 
Scheme  i.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Helpers: Qualification and Benefits under the Scheme i. The benefits of the Scheme will be provided to all the eligible beneficiaries through the 
platform of Anganwadi Centres by Anganwadi Workers (AWWs) and Anganwadi 
Helpers (AWHs) under the Scheme which considers the Anganwadi Workers and 
Anganwadi Helpers as “honorary workers” from the local community who come forward 
to render their services on part-time basis in the area of child care and development.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
in the area of child care and development. AWWs and AWHs are the grass root functionaries who shall implement the Scheme. ii. AWWs and AWHs being honorary workers shall be paid monthly honoraria as decided 
by Government from time to time. iii. Minimum prescribed qualification for AWW/AWH shall be 12th Standard and age limit 
shall be 18-35 years for engagement of AWWs and AWHs. Educational qualifications 
will apply to newly engaged AWW/AWH.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Educational qualifications will apply to newly engaged AWW/AWH. The retirement age of Anganwadi 
Workers/Helpers shall be decided by the concerned State Government/UT 
Administration. In any case, AWW/AWH must not be engaged beyond the age of 65 
years. iv.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
engaged beyond the age of 65 years. iv. 50% posts of Anganwadi Worker shall be filled by promotion of Anganwadi Helpers 
with 5 years of experience subject to availability of vacancies and fulfilment of 
educational qualifications, track record of service, additional qualification and age 
criteria. v.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
of service, additional qualification and age criteria. v. 50% posts of Supervisors shall be filled by promotion of Anganwadi Workers with 5 
years of experience subject to availability of vacancies having the prescribed 
educational qualifications as per the recruitment rules for the post of Supervisor failing 
which the vacancies shall be filled by direct recruitment. vi.

vacancies shall be filled by direct recruitment. vi. Extant reservation orders on the subject issued by the Government in respect of 
Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, Other Backward Classes, etc. shall be kept in 
view by the State Governments/UT Administrations while promoting the AWW/AWH. The remainder of the Section discusses the various services provided at the AWC to improve 
nutrition outcomes.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024

17  3.3 
Supplementary Nutrition  Supplementary Nutrition, one of the six components under Anganwadi Services, is provided 
to the beneficiaries of Anganwadi Services through the network of 14 lakh Anganwadi Centres 
located across the country. It is primarily designed to bridge the gap between the 
Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) and the Average Daily Intake (ADI).

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Allowance (RDA) and the Average Daily Intake (ADI). The National Food Security Act (NFSA), 2013 mandates provision of Supplementary Nutrition 
to every pregnant women and lactating mother up to 6 months after child birth, and every child 
in the age group of 6 months to 6 years (including those suffering from malnutrition). Place of serving/distribution: Supplementary Nutrition shall be provided at AWC during 
working hours.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
shall be provided at AWC during working hours. It shall be provided to registered beneficiaries who visit their respective AWCs. Pregnant women, lactating mothers and children up to the age of 6 years residing even 
temporarily in the coverage area of the AWC shall be registered and provided supplementary 
nutrition and other services.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
registered and provided supplementary nutrition and other services. In case the beneficiary is registered at another AWC, she/he shall 
be migrated to the new AWC during the period of stay through the migration facility in the 
Poshan Tracker.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
through the migration facility in the Poshan Tracker. Number of Days Supplementary Food to be Served: Supplementary Nutrition under the 
Scheme shall be served for a minimum of 300 days in a calendar year, i.e., on an average 25 
days in a month with respect to Morning Snacks, Hot Cooked Meals (HCM) and Take-Home 
Ration. Only those beneficiaries who are registered at the AWC are entitled to receive Supplementary 
Nutrition.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
the AWC are entitled to receive Supplementary Nutrition. At the time of registration, the beneficiary should be informed about their entitlement 
of Supplementary Nutrition, periodicity of distribution of Take-Home Ration (THR) and time of 
provision of Hot Cooked Meal (HCM) at the AWC.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
of Hot Cooked Meal (HCM) at the AWC. All pregnant women and lactating mothers (PW&LM), children in the age group of 6-36 
months, SAM children and adolescent girls in the age group of 14-18 years (wherever 
applicable) are entitled to receive THR and Children in the age group of 3-6 years are entitled 
to receive HCM and Morning Snacks under the Anganwadi Services from the AWC where 
they are registered.

preferences. 3.4 
Diet Diversity including Integration of Fortified Rice and Millets  Nutrition should aim at providing quality protein and essential micronutrients.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
aim at providing quality protein and essential micronutrients. Local dietary 
inputs and fresh produce (green vegetables, fruits, medicinal plants and herbs), fortified rice 
and millets shall be actively encouraged in prescription of meals to be served to different 
categories of beneficiaries under the Scheme to promote health, wellness and immunity and

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 
19  to manage anemia. Nuts and oilseeds like groundnut and sesame seeds provide energy, 
protein as well as fats. In addition, they are also a good source of nutrients like calcium, 
phosphorous, magnesium and some B-Complex Vitamins like Folic Acid, etc. Only jaggery 
should be used for sweetening; white sugar should not be used.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
for sweetening; white sugar should not be used. The food items for Take Home Ration (not raw ration) and Hot Cooked Meals (HCM) are 
specific to the States and shall include locally grown/available wholesome fruits and 
vegetables. Food ingredients used in Supplementary Nutrition shall comprise cereals, pulses, 
millets (coarse grains), nuts and some fat to increase the energy content.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
and some fat to increase the energy content. Millets should be 
mandatorily supplied at least once a week and suitably integrated in THR and HCM in a 
palatable form. 3.5 
Hot Cooked Meals  In order to encourage local dietary diversity and intake of fresh meals, prime focus of nutrition 
support shall be Hot Cooked Meals. The supplementary nutrition shall be provided in two 
servings, i.e., Morning Snacks and Hot Cooked Meal for beneficiaries.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Morning Snacks and Hot Cooked Meal for beneficiaries. 3.6 
Take Home Ration (not raw ration)  Take Home Ration must ensure dietary diversity and last mile delivery to the beneficiary. Take 
Home Ration (THR) shall be prepared using locally available and culturally appropriate food 
ingredients. THR shall not be given in the form of raw ration (Wheat, Rice, Dal etc.). States 
can provide Hot Cooked Meals (HCM) also to the beneficiaries in lieu of THR.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
also to the beneficiaries in lieu of THR. However, in 
case of SAM Children, additional nutrition has to be given in the form of THR. States/UTs need to clearly delineate the THR products that are being delivered to target 
beneficiaries based on their nutritional requirements such as children who are SAM, 
adolescent girls in the age group of 14-18 years, pregnant women and lactating mothers, 
besides children aged 6 months to 3 years.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
besides children aged 6 months to 3 years. All States/UTs shall revamp their respective websites in accordance with Mission Poshan 2.0 
and update with detailed information (including calorie, protein, micro-nutrients, energy etc.) 
on THR product, formats, production models and nutritional profiling across beneficiary 
groups.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 
20  3.7 
Periodicity of distribution of THR  THR shall be distributed regularly to registered beneficiaries. The beneficiaries in the 
categories of PW&LM, Children 6 months - 3 years are not expected to visit the AWCs daily. The distribution of THR to beneficiaries shall be made twice a month, preferably on 1st and 
15th of each calendar month uniformly.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
1st and 15th of each calendar month uniformly. If either of these days happens to be a holiday, THR 
should be given on the next working day. THR shall be properly tracked to last mile beneficiary and nutritional standards monitored to 
ensure both adequate quantity and quality standards of the meal. Tracking shall be done in 
accordance with the Streamlined Guidelines dated 13th January 2021 issued by MoWCD. 3.8 
Development of Community Food Habits  State Govts.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
in smaller volume of food to be promoted. 3.9 
Procurement of Supplementary Nutrition  State/UT must introduce transparent processes for procurement of Supplementary Nutrition 
as per GFR and vigilance guidelines and ensure that THR procured conforms to technical and 
nutritional standards set by MoWCD. Reference in this regard is invited to Streamlined 
Guidelines dated 13th January 2021.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 
21  Proper receipts and registers shall be maintained by the AWW for food grains supplied 
(Wheat, Fortified Rice, Millets, Coarse Grains, Dal and other edibles etc.) to the AWC. Oversight services and audits shall be conducted by Village Health Sanitation and Nutrition 
Committee (VHSNC), Lady Supervisor and CDPO on monthly basis.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
(VHSNC), Lady Supervisor and CDPO on monthly basis. The details of food grains 
allocation and utilization shall be monitored under the Poshan Tracker as and when the 
module for the same is developed by NeGD. 3.10 Quality Testing  States/UTs shall ensure the quality of Supplementary Nutrition – THR and HCM- are being 
provided with reference to the norms of food safety as well as nutrient composition.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
of food safety as well as nutrient composition. Supplementary Nutrition must conform to prescribed standards laid down under the Food 
Safety and Standards Act, 2006 and regulations made there under to ensure consistent 
quality and nutritive value per serving. Quality testing shall be done through Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) 
owned or authorized laboratories.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Authority of India (FSSAI) owned or authorized laboratories. State Governments and the Union Territory Administrations 
shall carry out periodic checks and get Supplementary Nutrition tested through the FSSAI 
owned/registered/empanelled/accredited laboratories for adhering to food quality standards 
and testing in consultation with FSSAI in accordance with Streamlined Guidelines issued by 
Central Government to ensure that the meal meets with the quality and nutritional standards 
specified.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
of checking for facilitating ease of doing business. Random testing shall be conducted by Anganwadi Services functionaries after receipt of stock 
at the AWC or at the Block level. Anganwadi Services functionaries i.e. CDPO or Supervisor 
shall draw the samples, as per the prescribed procedure and send the sample for testing to a 
FSSAI owned/registered/empanelled/NABL accredited laboratory.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
for testing to a FSSAI owned/registered/empanelled/NABL accredited laboratory. The District Nutrition 
Committee under the DM shall ensure periodic testing of THR in randomly selected AWCs. In 
case of Hot Cooked Meal, it should be ensured that it is prepared in proper kitchen sheds 
having adequate sanitation and safe drinking water so as to maintain hygienic conditions. LPG cylinder and gas stove should be used for cooking.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Saksham Anganwadi and Poshan 2.0 
22  3.11 Role of Panchayats  Panchayats shall play the continuing role in creation and management of Anganwadi 
Infrastructure. State Govts/MoPRI shall ensure active participation of Village Health Sanitation 
& Nutrition Committees (VHSNC), Mothers Groups etc., for regular monitoring and social audit 
and local community ownership of the programme under Poshan 2.0.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
community ownership of the programme under Poshan 2.0. The role of Panchayati Raj Institutions is very important for the success of nutritional 
interventions. The Panchayati Raj Department through Poshan Panchayats shall play a very 
active rote for malnutrition free India through Jan Andolans. Awareness generation on the 
effects of malnutrition at the Poshan Panchayat platform can be the first step.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
Poshan Panchayat platform can be the first step. At the 
grassroots level, Panchayats can serve as a useful convergence platform for Mothers Groups 
and VHSNC to discuss issues of nutrition and wellness and sensitize people about the 
importance of nutrition for beneficiaries. Functionaries of Anganwadis (AWW, AWH, ASHA 
and ANM) shall participate in such meetings.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
ASHA and ANM) shall participate in such meetings. The Panchayat shall also have the mandate of oversight services with regard to receipt of 
Supplementary Nutrition, quality thereof and distribution to all beneficiaries. POSHAN Panchayats and VHSNCs shall discuss the situation of malnutrition in the Gram 
Panchayat, including status of children affected by malnutrition, the probable causes of 
malnutrition and implementation gaps that need to be addressed.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
and implementation gaps that need to be addressed. Poshan Panchayats, Mothers’ Groups and VHSNCs must be strengthened to empower 
beneficiaries through oversight services and for transparency and accountability.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
through oversight services and for transparency and accountability. 3.11.1 
Outline of activities centered on Poshan Panchayats  Poshan Panchayats for mothers, husbands, members of the family:   Poshan Panchayats shall promote the uptake of nutrition seeking behaviors and 
practices like early initiation of breastfeeding (EIBF) and Exclusive Breastfeeding for 6 
months, complementary feeding (CF) for children after 6 months, promotion of dietary 
diversity (DD) and consumption of fresh and local food produce, and the need for IFA 
supplementation and consumption of iron/B12 rich foods for holistic 
Anemia 
Prevention.

title: Poshan2 Saksham Anganwadi Guidelines 2024
content:
of iron/B12 rich foods for holistic Anemia Prevention.  Increase awareness about access to basic health and nutrition services that are 
available at their local Anganwadi Center and improve engagement with service
